items.q: What distinguishes active transport from facilitated diffusion?
items.correct: Active transport requires energy (ATP) and can move substances against the concentration gradient.
items.misconception: Both move substances down a concentration gradient, but active transport is faster.
items.random_wrong: Active transport only happens in animals and facilitated diffusion only in plants.
items.why: Students don't grasp that active transport's key feature is going against the gradient, not just 'using energy'.

items.q: In osmosis, which direction does water move?
items.correct: From a region of higher water potential (less concentrated solution) to lower water potential.
items.misconception: Water moves from high solute concentration to low solute concentration.
items.random_wrong: Water moves towards the sugar because sugar attracts water molecules chemically.
items.why: Students get confused between solute concentration and water potential; they flip the direction because 'high concentration' feels like it should attract.

items.q: What drives water up the xylem in a tall tree?
items.correct: Transpiration pull — evaporation from leaves creates tension that pulls water up the continuous water column.
items.misconception: Roots actively pump water up to the leaves like a heart.
items.random_wrong: Capillary action alone is enough to lift water to the top of any tree.
items.why: Students assume there must be an active 'pump' system like circulation in animals; they miss that the evaporation at the top does the work.

items.q: Do roots actively pump water up to the leaves like a heart?
items.correct: No — water is pulled up the xylem by transpiration: evaporation from the leaves creates tension in the water column.
items.misconception: Roots pump water up the stem the way a heart pumps blood.
items.random_wrong: Capillary action alone lifts water to the top of any tree.
items.why: Plants have no pump; the cohesion-tension from evaporating water draws the continuous water column upward through the xylem.

items.q: Does the phloem carry water up from the roots?
items.correct: No — the phloem transports dissolved sugars (made in leaves) around the plant; the xylem carries water and minerals up from the roots.
items.misconception: Phloem carries water up from the roots to the leaves.
items.random_wrong: Phloem and xylem both carry only water in the same direction.
items.why: Students swap the two transport tissues; xylem = water/minerals upward, phloem = sugars (translocation) in either direction.
Correct answer: water through partially permeable membrane. “solute through membrane” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: energy from respiration. “a concentration gradient” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A potato strip placed in pure water gains mass. If it was 5.0 g and is now 5.6 g, what's the percentage change?
items.why: The real figure is 12 %. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
